topics.title: Amazon ECS — Containers Without the Cluster Headache
topics.description: ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's managed container orchestration platform. You define what Docker containers to run, how much CPU/memory they need, and how many copies — ECS handles scheduling, placement, health monitoring, and rolling deployments. With Fargate, you don't even manage the underlying EC2 instances.
topics.story: Think of ECS as a staffing agency for containers. You tell the agency: 'I need a Java developer (container) who needs a desk and a chair (512 CPU + 1GB RAM), and I always want 3 of them working at any time.' The agency (ECS) finds available office space (EC2 instances or Fargate), seats the developers, replaces them if they quit or get sick (health checks), and when you say 'upgrade to a better developer spec', the agency gradually swaps them in without shutting down the office (rolling deployment).
topics.analogy: Task Definition = the job description and contract (Docker image, CPU, memory, environment variables, port mappings). Task = a running instance of that definition (one or more containers). Service = a long-running supervisor that keeps N tasks alive and registers them with a load balancer. Cluster = the pool of resources (EC2 instances or Fargate capacity) where tasks run. Fargate = serverless launch type — no EC2 instances to manage, AWS provisions compute per task. EC2 launch type = you manage the EC2 instances in the cluster (more control, more ops work).
topics.memoryAnchor: ECS vs EKS: ECS is simpler, AWS-native, less configuration. EKS is Kubernetes — standard across clouds but more complex. Fargate: no EC2 management, charged per vCPU/second + GB/second. EC2 launch type: cheaper for sustained load (Reserved Instances), full control. Task Definition: specifies ONE deployment unit (can contain multiple containers — e.g., app + sidecar). Service: keeps the desired count of tasks running. Container Registry: ECR (Elastic Container Registry) stores your Docker images.
topics.firstPrinciples: Containers package an application and all its dependencies into an isolated, portable unit. ECS's scheduler assigns tasks to available capacity based on resource requirements (CPU/memory) and placement strategies. When a task dies (OOM kill, crash), the ECS service scheduler immediately starts a replacement — the desired count is maintained. Rolling deployment: ECS starts new tasks with the new definition, waits for them to be healthy, then stops old tasks — configurable via minimumHealthyPercent (how many old tasks to keep during deploy) and maximumPercent (how many total tasks can run simultaneously). Fargate abstracts the host — each task gets its own isolated compute slice, no noisy-neighbour problems.
topics.bruteForce: Without ECS: SSH into each EC2 instance, run docker pull, docker stop (old container), docker run (new container) — manual, error-prone, downtime during deploy, no automatic health restart. With ECS: update task definition → ECS performs rolling deploy across all instances automatically.
topics.optimizedApproach: Fargate for new projects — no patching EC2 instances, no capacity planning for the host. Use Spot Fargate for batch workloads (up to 70% cheaper). EC2 launch type when: you need GPU, specific instance types, or cost savings at scale with Reserved Instances. Use ECS Service Auto Scaling with Application Auto Scaling — scale task count based on ALB request count or CPU. Use Secrets Manager integration in task definitions (never pass secrets as plaintext env vars). Use ECS Exec for debugging running containers without exposing SSH.
topics.whenToUse: ECS: containerized web services, microservices, batch jobs, scheduled tasks (EventBridge → ECS task). EKS when: team already knows Kubernetes, multi-cloud portability needed, complex service mesh required. Fargate when: variable load, operations simplicity priority. EC2 launch type when: cost efficiency at scale, GPU workloads, custom AMIs.

examples.title: Task Definition, Service, and Fargate Deployment
examples.explanation: A task definition is an immutable blueprint — each update creates a new revision (family:1, family:2...). Two IAM roles: executionRole (ECS infrastructure — pulling images, writing logs) and taskRole (your application code — S3 reads, DynamoDB writes). Secrets are fetched by ECS from Secrets Manager at task startup and injected as environment variables — your application code reads them via System.getenv() with no special SDK. The startPeriod gives slow-starting apps (JVM, Spring Boot) time to initialise before health checks are evaluated. essential:true means if the main app container crashes, ECS stops and replaces the whole task.
examples.pseudocode:
ECS Service rolling deployment sequence:
  Trigger: service updated to use new task definition revision :v2
  Config: minimumHealthyPercent=50, maximumPercent=200
  
  Current state: 2 tasks running (v1, v1)
  
  Step 1: Launch 2 new tasks (v2, v2) — total: 4 tasks (200% of desired)
  Step 2: Wait for new tasks to pass health checks + register with ALB
  Step 3: Deregister old tasks from ALB (connection draining)
  Step 4: Stop old tasks (v1, v1) — total: 2 tasks (v2, v2)
  
  If new task fails health check:
    ECS does NOT stop old tasks (would violate minimumHealthyPercent)
    Deployment stalls → after deploymentCircuitBreaker threshold → rollback to v1
  
  Circuit breaker (automatic rollback):
    If N consecutive tasks fail → ECS automatically rolls back to last stable revision
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner API: ECS Service with 2 Fargate tasks behind ALB. Rolling deploy on every push to main. Secrets (DB password, JWT secret) from Secrets Manager. Auto scaling: 2→10 tasks based on ALB RequestCountPerTarget. Nightly batch job: EventBridge cron → ECS RunTask (Fargate) → processes spaced repetition queue → task exits when done.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  ECR[ECR: devlearner-api:v1.2.3] -->|pull on startup| Task[ECS Task: Fargate]
  SM[Secrets Manager] -->|inject env vars| Task
  Task --> App[Spring Boot:8080]
  App --> CW[CloudWatch Logs]
  SVC[ECS Service] -->|keep 2 tasks alive| Task
  SVC -->|register| ALB[Application Load Balancer]
  ALB --> Task
  HC[Health Check: /actuator/health] -->|unhealthy 3x| Stop[Stop + replace task]
  NewTD[New Task Definition :v2] --> RollingDeploy[Rolling Deploy]
  RollingDeploy -->|launch v2| SVC
  SVC -->|drain + stop v1| OldTask[Old task removed]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"New image pushed to ECR: devlearner-api:v1.2.4","state":"ECR stores image. CI/CD updates task definition to :v1.2.4 — creates revision :5"},{"step":2,"description":"ECS Service updated to use task definition :5","state":"Rolling deployment starts. minimumHealthyPercent=50, maximumPercent=200"},{"step":3,"description":"ECS launches 2 new Fargate tasks (revision :5)","state":"Each task: own ENI, own IP. ECS fetches DB_PASSWORD from Secrets Manager."},{"step":4,"description":"Spring Boot starts, health check: GET /actuator/health","state":"startPeriod=60s grace. After 60s: health check begins. t+30s: HEALTHY"},{"step":5,"description":"New tasks register with ALB target group","state":"ALB now has 4 targets: 2 old (v1.2.3) + 2 new (v1.2.4). Traffic distributed."},{"step":6,"description":"ECS deregisters old tasks from ALB, waits for connection drain (30s)","state":"Old tasks: no new requests. In-flight requests complete."},{"step":7,"description":"Old tasks stopped. Deployment complete.","state":"ECS Service: 2 healthy tasks running v1.2.4. Total deploy time: ~3 minutes."}]

examples.title: ECR, ECS Auto Scaling, and Scheduled Tasks
examples.code:
# ── ECR — Elastic Container Registry ─────────────────────────────────
# Create a repository
aws ecr create-repository \
  --repository-name devlearner-api \
  --image-scanning-configuration scanOnPush=true \
  --encryption-configuration encryptionType=AES256

# Authenticate Docker to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
  docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
  123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Build, tag, and push
docker build -t devlearner-api .
docker tag devlearner-api:latest \
  123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/devlearner-api:v1.2.4
docker push \
  123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/devlearner-api:v1.2.4

# Lifecycle policy: keep only last 10 tagged images (auto-delete old ones)
aws ecr put-lifecycle-policy \
  --repository-name devlearner-api \
  --lifecycle-policy-text '{
    "rules": [{
      "rulePriority": 1,
      "selection": {"tagStatus": "tagged", "tagPrefixList": ["v"], "countType": "imageCountMoreThan", "countNumber": 10},
      "action": {"type": "expire"}
    }]
  }'

# ── ECS Service Auto Scaling ──────────────────────────────────────────
# Register ECS service with Application Auto Scaling
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/devlearner-api-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --min-capacity 2 \
  --max-capacity 20

# Target tracking: maintain ALB 500 requests per task
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/devlearner-api-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --policy-name api-scale-on-request-count \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration '{
    "TargetValue": 500.0,
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
      "PredefinedMetricType": "ALBRequestCountPerTarget",
      "ResourceLabel": "app/my-alb/xxx/targetgroup/api-tg/yyy"
    },
    "ScaleInCooldown": 300,
    "ScaleOutCooldown": 60
  }'

# ── Scheduled ECS Task — nightly batch job ────────────────────────────
# EventBridge rule: trigger ECS task every day at 2am UTC
aws events put-rule \
  --name nightly-srs-job \
  --schedule-expression 'cron(0 2 * * ? *)' \
  --state ENABLED

aws events put-targets \
  --rule nightly-srs-job \
  --targets '[
    {
      "Id": "srs-processor",
      "Arn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123:cluster/my-cluster",
      "RoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123:role/EventBridgeECSRole",
      "EcsParameters": {
        "TaskDefinitionArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123:task-definition/srs-processor:3",
        "LaunchType": "FARGATE",
        "NetworkConfiguration": {
          "awsvpcConfiguration": {
            "Subnets": ["subnet-private-1a"],
            "SecurityGroups": ["sg-batch-jobs"],
            "AssignPublicIp": "DISABLED"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  ]'
examples.explanation: ECR is AWS's private Docker registry — images stored close to ECS, no egress fees for pulling within the same region. scanOnPush enables automatic vulnerability scanning (powered by Snyk/Trivy) — view findings in ECR console or Security Hub. Lifecycle policies prevent unbounded storage growth. ECS Auto Scaling uses Application Auto Scaling (same service as DynamoDB auto scaling) — Target Tracking maintains a target metric, Scale Out Cooldown (60s) lets new tasks absorb load quickly, Scale In Cooldown (300s) prevents premature scale-down during traffic spikes. Scheduled tasks via EventBridge are the 'cron job' equivalent — run a task once at a specified time, pay only for the Fargate CPU/memory during execution.
examples.pseudocode:
ECS Auto Scaling target tracking:
  Target: ALBRequestCountPerTarget = 500
  Current state: 2 tasks, 800 requests/min each (800 > 500)
  
  Scale out calculation:
    Desired tasks = ceil(total_requests / target)
                  = ceil(1600 / 500) = 4 tasks
  
  ECS auto scaling:
    Increase desired count from 2 to 4
    ScaleOutCooldown: wait 60s before scaling again
    ECS launches 2 new Fargate tasks
    ALB distributes to 4 tasks: 400 req/min each (< 500 target)
  
  Traffic decreases: 400 req/min total → 100 per task
    Scale in calculation: ceil(400/500) = 1 task
    ScaleInCooldown: wait 300s (protects against spiky traffic)
    After 300s sustained low traffic → scale in to 2 (min capacity)
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner production: ECR repo with scan-on-push (CVE alerts), lifecycle policy keeping 10 versions. ECS Service auto scales from 2 to 20 Fargate tasks based on ALB request count. Nightly batch: EventBridge triggers ECS RunTask for SpacedRepetitionProcessor — updates all users' next-review dates, exits on completion. Cost: Fargate task running 30 minutes nightly for a batch job = ~$0.006/night.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  GH[GitHub Push] --> CICD[CI/CD Pipeline]
  CICD --> Build[docker build]
  Build --> Push[docker push to ECR]
  Push -->|new tag :v1.2.4| ECR[ECR Repository]
  ECR -->|scanOnPush| Scan[Vulnerability Scan]
  Scan -->|CVEs found| Alert[Security Hub Alert]
  Push --> Deploy[ECS Update Service]
  Deploy --> AS[ECS Auto Scaling]
  AS -->|ALB req/task > 500| ScaleOut[Scale 2 → 4 tasks]
  AS -->|ALB req/task < 100 for 300s| ScaleIn[Scale 4 → 2 tasks]
  EB[EventBridge: cron 2am] --> RunTask[ECS RunTask Fargate]
  RunTask --> Batch[SRS Processor Task]
  Batch -->|job done, exit 0| Stop[Task terminates]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"GitHub Actions: docker build + push to ECR: devlearner-api:v1.2.4","state":"ECR stores image. Vulnerability scan: 0 critical CVEs."},{"step":2,"description":"Pipeline registers new task definition revision :5 (image updated to v1.2.4)","state":"Task definition devlearner-api:5 created"},{"step":3,"description":"ECS Service updated → rolling deploy (previous steps already traced)","state":"2 tasks running v1.2.4"},{"step":4,"description":"Traffic spike: 2000 requests/minute (1000 per task)","state":"ALBRequestCountPerTarget = 1000 > 500. Scale out triggered."},{"step":5,"description":"Auto Scaling calculates desired: ceil(2000/500) = 4 tasks","state":"ECS launches 2 new Fargate tasks. ScaleOutCooldown=60s starts."},{"step":6,"description":"EventBridge fires at 2am UTC","state":"ECS RunTask: SRS Processor (Fargate, private subnet, no public IP)"},{"step":7,"description":"SRS Processor runs for 8 minutes, updates 50,000 user SRS records, exits 0","state":"Task terminated. Cost: 0.25 vCPU × 8min × $0.04048/hr = ~$0.001"}]

problems.title: Debug ECS Task that Keeps Restarting
problems.description: An ECS Fargate task starts, runs for 20 seconds, then stops. ECS immediately restarts it (because the service's desired count is 2). It keeps cycling. List the 4 most likely causes and how to diagnose each.
constraints: Health check: HTTP GET /health, interval=10s, retries=3

examples.output: Cause 1: Missing startPeriod — health checks fire at t=10s, Spring Boot not ready, 3 failures in 30s → task killed. Fix: add startPeriod=60. Cause 2: OOM kill — memory limit too low, JVM exceeds it. Check: CloudWatch Logs for OOMKilled. Cause 3: App crash (exception in startup). Check: CloudWatch Logs stream for the task. Cause 4: Secret not found — Secrets Manager valueFrom ARN wrong, ECS can't inject secret, task fails to start.
examples.explanation: The 20s cycle matches 3 health check failures (10s interval × 2 retries) without startPeriod — the most common cause

hints: aws ecs describe-tasks shows stopCode and stoppedReason
CloudWatch Logs: /ecs/{task-family} group, look for exit code and error messages
ECS console → Service → Events tab shows deployment and task stop reasons

problems.title: Design ECS Architecture for Microservices
problems.description: Design ECS architecture for a 3-service application: UserService (read-heavy, stateless), OrderService (write-heavy, moderate traffic), NotificationService (low traffic, send emails). Each service has its own container. Requirements: independent scaling, shared ALB with path routing, secrets managed securely, nightly batch for notifications.
constraints: Each service: separate task definition, separate ECS service
Secrets in Secrets Manager, never plaintext env vars

examples.output: 1 ALB, 3 listener rules (/api/users/*, /api/orders/*, /api/notifications/*) → 3 target groups → 3 ECS Services. UserService: min=4,max=40 (read-heavy). OrderService: min=2,max=20. NotificationService: min=1,max=5 + EventBridge scheduled task for nightly batch. Each task definition: separate taskRoleArn (least privilege).
examples.explanation: Each ECS Service has its own auto scaling policy tuned to its traffic pattern. Shared ALB saves ~$16/month vs 3 separate ALBs

hints: ALB listener rules with path-pattern conditions route to different target groups
Task role least privilege: UserService only needs DynamoDB read, OrderService needs read+write
NotificationService long-running service handles webhooks; scheduled ECS task handles bulk nightly sends

topics.title: AWS CodePipeline — CI/CD on AWS
topics.description: CodePipeline is AWS's fully managed CI/CD orchestration service. It automates the build, test, and deploy workflow — from a code commit in GitHub to a running ECS service update — with built-in integration for CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, Lambda, ECS, S3, and manual approval gates.
topics.story: Imagine an assembly line at a car factory. Raw materials (source code) enter one end. Each station on the assembly line performs one job — weld the frame (build), run quality tests (test), paint (staging deploy), final inspection (manual approval), ship to dealer (production deploy). CodePipeline IS the assembly line — it defines the stations, moves the car from one station to the next, and stops the line if any station fails. You don't manually drive the car between stations; the pipeline does.
topics.analogy: Pipeline = the end-to-end workflow definition. Stage = a phase of the pipeline (Source, Build, Test, Deploy). Action = a specific task within a stage (run CodeBuild, deploy to ECS). Artifact = the output of a stage passed to the next (source ZIP, built Docker image, CloudFormation template). Transition = the connection between stages. Manual Approval Action = a human gate — someone must click Approve in the console or respond to an SNS notification.
topics.memoryAnchor: CodePipeline is an orchestrator, not an executor. It coordinates other services: CodeBuild for building/testing, CodeDeploy for deployment strategies, ECR for image storage. Pipeline execution is triggered by source changes (GitHub webhook, CodeCommit push, S3 upload). Each stage must complete before the next begins. A failed action stops the pipeline (unless you configure it to continue). Cost: $1/month per active pipeline (+ underlying service costs).
topics.firstPrinciples: CI/CD is about removing human toil from the path between a code commit and running software. CodePipeline encodes your release process as configuration — the pipeline definition is infrastructure as code (stored in CloudFormation/CDK). Every stage execution is logged and auditable. Pipeline state is versioned — you can see the history of every deploy. Artifacts are passed between stages via S3 (CodePipeline manages the bucket). The pipeline model enforces linear progression by default (Source → Build → Deploy) but supports parallel actions within a stage and fan-in/fan-out between stages.
topics.bruteForce: Without CI/CD: developer SSHes into server, runs git pull, mvn package, copies JAR, restarts service manually. Human error-prone, not reproducible, no audit trail, downtime during restart. With CodePipeline: every commit triggers the pipeline automatically, builds in a clean environment (CodeBuild), deploys with zero downtime (ECS rolling deploy), audit trail in CloudTrail.
topics.optimizedApproach: Store pipeline definition in CloudFormation/CDK — treat the pipeline itself as code. Use CodeBuild cache (Docker layer cache, Maven .m2 cache in S3) to reduce build times. Use manual approval stages for production — require sign-off before prod deploy. Use CodePipeline notifications (SNS → Slack) for stage success/failure. Use parallel stages for independent tests (unit tests + integration tests in parallel reduces total pipeline time). Connect to GitHub via CodeStar Connections (OAuth app) — more reliable than CodeCommit webhooks.
topics.whenToUse: CodePipeline: AWS-native CI/CD, especially when deploying to ECS/Lambda/Elastic Beanstalk/CloudFormation. GitHub Actions is often simpler for pure CI and non-AWS deployments. CodePipeline shines for multi-stage pipelines with AWS-native deploy steps (ECS blue/green, CloudFormation change sets, Lambda deploy). Use both: GitHub Actions for CI (build + test), CodePipeline for CD (deploy to AWS environments).

examples.title: Full Pipeline — Source, Build, and ECS Deploy
examples.code:
# CloudFormation: Full CI/CD pipeline
# Source: GitHub → Build: CodeBuild → Deploy: ECS rolling update
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'

Resources:
  # ── S3 Artifact Bucket ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  ArtifactBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      VersioningConfiguration:
        Status: Enabled
      LifecycleConfiguration:
        Rules:
          - Status: Enabled
            ExpirationInDays: 30  # Auto-delete old artifacts

  # ── CodeBuild Project ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  BuildProject:
    Type: AWS::CodeBuild::Project
    Properties:
      Name: devlearner-api-build
      ServiceRole: !GetAtt CodeBuildRole.Arn
      Environment:
        Type: LINUX_CONTAINER
        ComputeType: BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL  # 3 GB RAM, 2 vCPU
        Image: aws/codebuild/standard:7.0
        PrivilegedMode: true  # Required for Docker builds
        EnvironmentVariables:
          - Name: ECR_REPO
            Value: !Sub '${AWS::AccountId}.dkr.ecr.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/devlearner-api'
          - Name: ECS_CLUSTER
            Value: my-cluster
          - Name: ECS_SERVICE
            Value: devlearner-api-service
      Source:
        Type: CODEPIPELINE
        BuildSpec: |
          version: 0.2
          phases:
            pre_build:
              commands:
                - echo Logging in to Amazon ECR...
                - aws ecr get-login-password | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin $ECR_REPO
                - IMAGE_TAG=$(echo $CODEBUILD_RESOLVED_SOURCE_VERSION | cut -c1-8)
                - IMAGE_URI=$ECR_REPO:$IMAGE_TAG
            build:
              commands:
                - echo Building the Docker image...
                - docker build -t $IMAGE_URI .
                - docker push $IMAGE_URI
                # Generate imagedefinitions.json for ECS deploy action
                - printf '[{"name":"api","imageUri":"%s"}]' $IMAGE_URI > imagedefinitions.json
            post_build:
              commands:
                - echo Build completed
          artifacts:
            files:
              - imagedefinitions.json  # Passed to ECS deploy stage
      Cache:
        Type: LOCAL
        Modes:
          - LOCAL_DOCKER_LAYER_CACHE  # Cache Docker layers — 30-60s faster rebuilds
          - LOCAL_SOURCE_CACHE

  # ── CodePipeline ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Pipeline:
    Type: AWS::CodePipeline::Pipeline
    Properties:
      Name: devlearner-api-pipeline
      RoleArn: !GetAtt PipelineRole.Arn
      ArtifactStore:
        Type: S3
        Location: !Ref ArtifactBucket
      Stages:
        # Stage 1: Source — fetch code from GitHub on push to main
        - Name: Source
          Actions:
            - Name: GitHub-Source
              ActionTypeId:
                Category: Source
                Owner: AWS
                Provider: CodeStarSourceConnection
                Version: '1'
              Configuration:
                ConnectionArn: !Ref GitHubConnection
                FullRepositoryId: myorg/devlearner
                BranchName: main
                OutputArtifactFormat: CODE_ZIP
              OutputArtifacts:
                - Name: SourceOutput
        
        # Stage 2: Build — run CodeBuild
        - Name: Build
          Actions:
            - Name: Build-and-Push
              ActionTypeId:
                Category: Build
                Owner: AWS
                Provider: CodeBuild
                Version: '1'
              Configuration:
                ProjectName: !Ref BuildProject
              InputArtifacts:
                - Name: SourceOutput
              OutputArtifacts:
                - Name: BuildOutput  # Contains imagedefinitions.json
        
        # Stage 3: Manual approval for production
        - Name: Approve-Production
          Actions:
            - Name: Manual-Approval
              ActionTypeId:
                Category: Approval
                Owner: AWS
                Provider: Manual
                Version: '1'
              Configuration:
                NotificationArn: !Ref ApprovalSNSTopic
                CustomData: 'New build ready. Review CloudWatch metrics before approving.'
        
        # Stage 4: Deploy to ECS
        - Name: Deploy
          Actions:
            - Name: ECS-Deploy
              ActionTypeId:
                Category: Deploy
                Owner: AWS
                Provider: ECS
                Version: '1'
              Configuration:
                ClusterName: my-cluster
                ServiceName: devlearner-api-service
                FileName: imagedefinitions.json  # Maps container name → new image URI
              InputArtifacts:
                - Name: BuildOutput
examples.explanation: The pipeline has 4 stages: Source (GitHub webhook triggers on push to main), Build (CodeBuild builds Docker image, pushes to ECR, outputs imagedefinitions.json), Approve (human gate — production deploys require approval), Deploy (ECS action reads imagedefinitions.json, registers new task definition, triggers ECS rolling update). The imagedefinitions.json file is the contract between CodeBuild and the ECS deploy action — it maps container names to new image URIs. Docker layer cache in CodeBuild significantly reduces build times on incremental changes.
examples.pseudocode:
Pipeline execution flow:
  1. git push to main branch
  2. GitHub sends webhook to CodeStar Connection
  3. Pipeline: Source stage starts
     → Downloads repo ZIP to S3 artifact bucket
     → SourceOutput artifact created
  4. Pipeline: Build stage starts
     → CodeBuild receives SourceOutput
     → Runs buildspec.yml phases
     → Docker build (5 mins) + push to ECR
     → Creates imagedefinitions.json: [{"name":"api","imageUri":"...ecr.../devlearner-api:abc1234"}]
     → BuildOutput artifact (imagedefinitions.json) uploaded to S3
  5. Pipeline: Approve-Production stage
     → SNS notification sent to on-call engineer
     → Pipeline PAUSED (waits up to 7 days)
     → Engineer clicks Approve in console
  6. Pipeline: Deploy stage
     → ECS action reads imagedefinitions.json
     → Calls ECS RegisterTaskDefinition (new revision with updated image)
     → Calls ECS UpdateService (triggers rolling deploy)
     → Waits for ECS service to reach STEADY_STATE
     → Pipeline: SUCCESS
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner full pipeline: GitHub push → CodeBuild (mvn test + docker build + push to ECR) → Manual approval (Slack message via SNS) → ECS rolling deploy. Build time: ~4 minutes (Maven cache in S3 reduces from 8 minutes). Rollback: click 'Release change' on previous pipeline execution or update task definition via ECS console. Add a pre-deploy Lambda stage for smoke tests against staging before approval.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  GH[GitHub: push to main] -->|webhook| CP[CodePipeline]
  CP --> S1[Stage 1: Source]
  S1 -->|SourceOutput ZIP| S2[Stage 2: Build]
  S2 -->|CodeBuild| B[docker build + push ECR]
  B -->|imagedefinitions.json| S3[Stage 3: Manual Approval]
  S3 -->|SNS notification| Eng[Engineer]
  Eng -->|clicks Approve| S4[Stage 4: Deploy]
  S4 -->|ECS UpdateService| ECS[ECS Rolling Deploy]
  ECS --> Done[Pipeline: SUCCEEDED]
  B -->|build fails| Fail[Pipeline: FAILED, no deploy]
  Eng -->|clicks Reject| Reject[Pipeline: REJECTED, no deploy]

Build total: 3m 42s"},{"step":5,"description":"imagedefinitions.json created: [{name:api, imageUri:123.ecr.../devlearner-api:abc1234f}]","state":"BuildOutput artifact uploaded to S3"},{"step":6,"description":"Manual Approval stage: pipeline pauses. SNS sends to Slack channel #deploys","state":"Message: 'New build abc1234f ready. Review CloudWatch metrics before approving.'"},{"step":7,"description":"Engineer reviews metrics (no error spike), clicks Approve in CodePipeline console","state":"ECS deploy action reads imagedefinitions.json → ECS rolling update → SUCCEEDED in 3m"}]

problems.title: Fix Failing CodeBuild Build
problems.description: A CodeBuild build fails at the Docker push step with 'no basic auth credentials'. The build log shows the ECR login command ran but the push still fails. Identify the two possible causes and fix each.
constraints: ECR repo is in us-east-1, CodeBuild project is in us-east-1

examples.input: docker push fails: no basic auth credentials
examples.output: Cause 1: CodeBuild service role missing ecr:GetAuthorizationToken + ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability + ecr:PutImage permissions. Fix: attach ECR push policy to CodeBuild role. Cause 2: ECR login command output not piped correctly — 'aws ecr get-login-password | docker login' must use --password-stdin, not the old get-login method.
examples.explanation: The old 'aws ecr get-login' returns a 'docker login ...' command to eval — the new method pipes the password to stdin. Both work but the new method is required in newer AWS CLI versions

hints: CodeBuild IAM role needs: ecr:GetAuthorizationToken, ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability, ecr:InitiateLayerUpload, ecr:UploadLayerPart, ecr:CompleteLayerUpload, ecr:PutImage
Managed policy: AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPowerUser covers all ECR push operations
PrivilegedMode: true is required for docker build/push — without it, Docker daemon is not available

problems.title: Design a Multi-Environment Pipeline
problems.description: Design a CodePipeline that deploys to 3 environments: dev (auto-deploy on every push), staging (auto-deploy after tests pass), prod (manual approval required). Each environment is a separate ECS cluster. Add automated integration tests between staging and production approval.
constraints: Integration tests: HTTP requests to staging API, assert 200s
Rollback: prod should roll back automatically if post-deploy health check fails

examples.input: Source → Build → Deploy Dev → Integration Tests → Manual Approval → Deploy Prod
examples.output: Stage 1: Source (GitHub main). Stage 2: Build + unit tests (CodeBuild). Stage 3: Deploy to Dev ECS. Stage 4: Integration Tests (CodeBuild runs newman/pytest against dev URL). Stage 5: Deploy to Staging ECS. Stage 6: Integration Tests against staging. Stage 7: Manual Approval. Stage 8: Deploy to Prod ECS.
examples.explanation: Integration tests as CodeBuild action in the pipeline: if tests fail (non-zero exit code), pipeline stops, prod never gets the bad build

hints: Test stage: CodeBuild project that runs pytest/newman against the deployed URL, exit 1 on failure
ECS CodeDeploy blue/green (via CodeDeploy) adds automatic rollback if post-deploy canary test fails
Pipeline can have multiple actions in one stage running in parallel (e.g., unit tests + static analysis simultaneously)

topics.title: CloudFormation and CloudWatch — Infrastructure as Code and Observability
topics.description: CloudFormation lets you define your entire AWS infrastructure in YAML/JSON templates — VPCs, ECS services, RDS databases, IAM roles — and provision it repeatably and safely. CloudWatch is AWS's unified observability platform: logs, metrics, alarms, and dashboards for everything running in your account.
topics.story: CloudFormation is like an architect's blueprint for a building. Without blueprints, every builder improvises — buildings come out differently, can't be reproduced. With blueprints (CloudFormation), you hand the plans to AWS and it builds the exact same building every time. If you change the blueprint (update the stack), CloudFormation figures out the minimum set of changes needed — it doesn't tear down the whole building to repaint one wall. CloudWatch is the building's monitoring system — smoke detectors, temperature sensors, and security cameras. You set thresholds (alarms), and when a sensor triggers, the alarm notifies you (SNS) or automatically acts (Auto Scaling).
topics.analogy: Template = the infrastructure blueprint (YAML/JSON file, stored in S3 or version control). Stack = a deployed instance of a template (all the AWS resources it created). Change Set = a preview of what will change before you apply an update (like a dry run). Drift = when someone manually changed a resource outside CloudFormation (stack is out of sync with reality). CloudWatch Metric = a time-series data point (CPU%, request count, error count). CloudWatch Alarm = a rule that triggers when a metric crosses a threshold. CloudWatch Logs = log streams from Lambda, ECS, EC2, API Gateway centralised in one place. CloudWatch Dashboard = a visual panel of multiple metrics and alarms.
topics.memoryAnchor: CloudFormation: stack = group of AWS resources. Template sections: AWSTemplateFormatVersion, Description, Parameters, Mappings, Conditions, Resources (required), Outputs. !Ref = reference another resource. !Sub = string substitution. !GetAtt = get an attribute of a resource. CloudWatch: metric retention: 15 months. Alarm states: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA. Log retention: configurable (default: never expire — you pay forever unless you set a retention policy).
topics.firstPrinciples: CloudFormation uses a declarative model: you describe the desired state, not the steps to get there. CloudFormation's engine computes the dependency graph from resource references (a Security Group depends on a VPC), determines creation order, and manages parallel creation where possible. During updates, CloudFormation computes a diff and applies only the changed properties — some changes are in-place (update security group rules), others require replacement (change EC2 instance type = terminate + launch new). CloudWatch metrics are collected by CloudWatch agents or AWS services directly — no code changes needed for infrastructure metrics. Custom metrics require your application to call PutMetricData. Alarms evaluate metric data points against a threshold over a period (e.g., CPUUtilization > 80% for 3 out of 5 data points).
topics.bruteForce: Without CloudFormation: click through AWS console to create resources. Steps are not recorded, not reproducible, not reviewable. Someone deletes a security group by accident — you rebuild from memory. With CloudFormation: template in Git, PR review, 'aws cloudformation deploy' creates everything deterministically. Deleted resource? Delete the stack and recreate.
topics.optimizedApproach: Organise stacks by lifecycle: network stack (VPC, subnets — rarely changes), data stack (RDS, DynamoDB — changes sometimes), app stack (ECS service, ALB — changes often). Use cross-stack references (Exports/ImportValue) to share resources between stacks. Use CloudFormation StackSets for multi-account/multi-region deployments. For CloudWatch: set log retention on all log groups (7-90 days for dev, 1-2 years for prod). Create a dashboard with your key SLIs (error rate, p99 latency, task count) as the first thing you check every morning. Use Composite Alarms to reduce alert noise — 'alert me only if BOTH error rate is high AND latency is high'.
topics.whenToUse: CloudFormation: defining and deploying AWS infrastructure reproducibly. Terraform is an alternative (multi-cloud, larger community, HCL language). CDK (Cloud Development Kit) lets you write CloudFormation in TypeScript/Python/Java — generates CloudFormation under the hood. CloudWatch: all AWS workloads — it's the default monitoring for every AWS service. Use Datadog/Grafana for cross-cloud observability or richer dashboards.

examples.title: CloudFormation — Parameters, Outputs, and Cross-Stack References
examples.code:
# ── Network Stack (deployed once, rarely changes) ──────────────────────
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: 'Shared VPC and subnets for all services'

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, staging, prod]
    Default: dev
  VpcCidr:
    Type: String
    Default: '10.0.0.0/16'

Conditions:
  IsProd: !Equals [!Ref Environment, prod]

Resources:
  VPC:
    Type: AWS::EC2::VPC
    Properties:
      CidrBlock: !Ref VpcCidr
      EnableDnsHostnames: true
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: !Sub '${Environment}-vpc'

  PublicSubnet1:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Subnet
    Properties:
      VpcId: !Ref VPC
      CidrBlock: !Select [0, !Cidr [!Ref VpcCidr, 6, 8]]  # First /24
      AvailabilityZone: !Select [0, !GetAZs '']
      MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true

  # NAT Gateway: only create in prod (saves ~$35/month in dev)
  NATGateway:
    Type: AWS::EC2::NatGateway
    Condition: IsProd  # Only created when Environment=prod
    Properties:
      SubnetId: !Ref PublicSubnet1
      AllocationId: !GetAtt ElasticIP.AllocationId

  ElasticIP:
    Type: AWS::EC2::EIP
    Condition: IsProd
    Properties:
      Domain: vpc

Outputs:
  # Exported values can be imported by other stacks
  VPCId:
    Value: !Ref VPC
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${Environment}-vpc-id'
  
  PublicSubnet1Id:
    Value: !Ref PublicSubnet1
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${Environment}-public-subnet-1'

---
# ── App Stack — imports from Network Stack ─────────────────────────────
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    Default: dev

Resources:
  ALB:
    Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::LoadBalancer
    Properties:
      Name: !Sub '${Environment}-alb'
      Subnets:
        # Import from Network Stack — decoupled but connected
        - !ImportValue !Sub '${Environment}-public-subnet-1'
      SecurityGroups: [!Ref ALBSecurityGroup]

  ALBSecurityGroup:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
    Properties:
      GroupDescription: ALB security group
      VpcId: !ImportValue !Sub '${Environment}-vpc-id'
      SecurityGroupIngress:
        - IpProtocol: tcp
          FromPort: 443
          ToPort: 443
          CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0

  ECSCluster:
    Type: AWS::ECS::Cluster
    Properties:
      ClusterName: !Sub '${Environment}-cluster'
      CapacityProviders:
        - FARGATE
        - FARGATE_SPOT  # Enable Spot for cost savings
      DefaultCapacityProviderStrategy:
        - CapacityProvider: FARGATE
          Weight: 1
          Base: 1  # At least 1 task on regular Fargate (reliability)
        - CapacityProvider: FARGATE_SPOT
          Weight: 4  # 80% of tasks on Spot (cost savings)
examples.explanation: Parameters make templates reusable across environments — deploy the same template with Environment=dev or Environment=prod. Conditions (IsProd) enable conditional resource creation — NAT Gateway only in prod, saving $35/month in dev. Outputs + Exports + ImportValue create loose coupling between stacks — the App Stack doesn't need to know HOW the VPC was created, only its ID. If the Network Stack is updated (new subnet added), it doesn't force an App Stack update. FARGATE_SPOT mixes regular Fargate (Base=1, always available) with Spot (Weight=4, 80% cheaper, may be interrupted) — perfect for stateless services that can handle occasional task interruption.
examples.pseudocode:
CloudFormation stack update process:
  Template change: add new security group rule
  
  1. aws cloudformation deploy --template-file app.yaml --stack-name prod-app
  2. CloudFormation computes change set:
     Modified: ALBSecurityGroup (SecurityGroupIngress change)
     Unchanged: ALB, ECSCluster, ... (no update needed)
  3. Apply changes:
     ALBSecurityGroup: in-place update (SG rule change doesn't require replacement)
  
  Template change: modify ECS cluster name
  1. CloudFormation computes change set:
     Requires Replacement: ECSCluster (ClusterName is immutable)
  2. CloudFormation warns: this change will DELETE and RECREATE ECSCluster
     → Replacement creates new cluster first, then deletes old
     → Any services on old cluster must be moved manually
  
  Rollback on failure:
    If any resource creation/update fails:
    → CloudFormation rolls back ALL changes in the update
    → Stack reverts to last stable state
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner infrastructure: network-stack.yaml (VPC, subnets, IGW), data-stack.yaml (RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache), app-stack.yaml (ECS cluster, ALB, ECS service). CodePipeline deploys app-stack.yaml on every build. Network and data stacks deploy manually (rare changes). Separate stacks per environment: dev-app-stack, staging-app-stack, prod-app-stack — deploy the same template with different parameter values.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  CF[CloudFormation Template YAML] -->|aws cloudformation deploy| CS[Create Change Set]
  CS --> Preview{Review Changes}
  Preview -->|approve| Apply[Apply Change Set]
  Apply --> Resources[AWS Resources Created/Updated]
  Resources --> CW[CloudWatch monitors]
  
  NS[Network Stack: VPC, Subnets] -->|Export: dev-vpc-id| AS[App Stack]
  AS -->|ImportValue: dev-vpc-id| ALB2[ALB in VPC]
  
  Prod[Environment=prod] -->|Condition: IsProd=true| NAT[NAT Gateway created]
  Dev[Environment=dev] -->|Condition: IsProd=false| NoNAT[NAT Gateway skipped]
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"Deploy network stack: aws cloudformation deploy --stack-name dev-network","state":"CloudFormation creates: VPC (10.0.0.0/16), PublicSubnet1 (10.0.0.0/24), IGW. IsProd=false: NAT Gateway skipped."},{"step":2,"description":"Network stack completes. Outputs exported.","state":"Export 'dev-vpc-id' = vpc-abc123. Export 'dev-public-subnet-1' = subnet-def456"},{"step":3,"description":"Deploy app stack: aws cloudformation deploy --stack-name dev-app","state":"ALB: ImportValue 'dev-public-subnet-1' → subnet-def456. Security Group: ImportValue 'dev-vpc-id' → vpc-abc123"},{"step":4,"description":"App stack update: change ECS task definition image tag","state":"Change set: Modified=ECSService (rolling update). No replacement. Safe to apply."},{"step":5,"description":"CloudFormation applies change set: ECS UpdateService called","state":"ECS rolling deploy starts. CloudFormation polls until STEADY_STATE."},{"step":6,"description":"Update fails: new container crashes on startup (OOM)","state":"CloudFormation rollback: ECS UpdateService with previous task definition. Stack reverts to last stable state."}]

examples.title: CloudWatch — Metrics, Alarms, Dashboards, and Log Insights
examples.code:
import boto3
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

cw = boto3.client('cloudwatch')
logs = boto3.client('logs')

# ── Custom Metric — publish application-level metrics ─────────────────
def record_business_metric(metric_name, value, unit='Count', dimensions=None):
    """
    Publish a custom metric to CloudWatch.
    Use cases: order count, payment failures, active users, SRS queue size.
    Cost: $0.30 per custom metric per month.
    """
    cw.put_metric_data(
        Namespace='DevLearner/Application',  # Custom namespace groups metrics
        MetricData=[
            {
                'MetricName': metric_name,
                'Value': value,
                'Unit': unit,
                'Timestamp': datetime.now(timezone.utc),
                'Dimensions': dimensions or [
                    {'Name': 'Environment', 'Value': 'prod'}
                ]
            }
        ]
    )

# In your application code:
# record_business_metric('OrdersProcessed', 1)
# record_business_metric('PaymentFailures', 1, dimensions=[{'Name':'Reason','Value':'InsufficientFunds'}])
# record_business_metric('SRSQueueDepth', len(pending_items), unit='Count')

# ── CloudWatch Alarm — alert on high error rate ────────────────────────
def create_error_rate_alarm(api_name, threshold_percent=1.0):
    """Alert if 5xx rate > 1% over 5 minutes."""
    cw.put_metric_alarm(
        AlarmName=f'{api_name}-high-error-rate',
        AlarmDescription='5xx error rate exceeded 1% — investigate immediately',
        MetricName='5XXError',
        Namespace='AWS/ApiGateway',
        Dimensions=[{'Name': 'ApiName', 'Value': api_name}],
        Statistic='Sum',
        Period=300,           # 5-minute evaluation window
        EvaluationPeriods=2,  # Alert if threshold breached for 2 consecutive periods
        Threshold=threshold_percent,
        ComparisonOperator='GreaterThanThreshold',
        TreatMissingData='notBreaching',  # No data = assume OK (not ALARM)
        AlarmActions=[
            'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123:pagerduty-alerts'  # SNS → PagerDuty
        ],
        OKActions=[
            'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123:slack-notifications'  # Alert when recovered
        ]
    )

# ── Composite Alarm — reduce noise with AND logic ─────────────────────
cw.put_composite_alarm(
    AlarmName='prod-critical-outage',
    AlarmDescription='Alert only when BOTH high errors AND high latency (reduces false positives)',
    AlarmRule='ALARM("api-high-error-rate") AND ALARM("api-high-latency")',
    AlarmActions=['arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123:pagerduty-alerts']
)

# ── CloudWatch Logs Insights — query logs like SQL ────────────────────
def find_slow_requests(log_group, hours_back=1):
    """Find API requests slower than 2000ms in the last hour."""
    end_time = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    start_time = end_time - timedelta(hours=hours_back)
    
    query = """
        fields @timestamp, @message
        | filter @message like /duration/
        | parse @message /duration=(?<duration_ms>[0-9]+)/
        | filter duration_ms > 2000
        | sort duration_ms desc
        | limit 50
    """
    
    response = logs.start_query(
        logGroupName=log_group,
        startTime=int(start_time.timestamp()),
        endTime=int(end_time.timestamp()),
        queryString=query
    )
    
    query_id = response['queryId']
    # Poll for results
    import time
    while True:
        results = logs.get_query_results(queryId=query_id)
        if results['status'] in ('Complete', 'Failed', 'Cancelled'):
            break
        time.sleep(1)
    
    return results['results']

# ── Set Log Retention — avoid infinite storage costs ──────────────────
def set_log_retention(log_group_name, retention_days=30):
    logs.put_retention_policy(
        logGroupName=log_group_name,
        retentionInDays=retention_days  # 1,3,5,7,14,30,60,90,120,150,180,365,400,545,731,1827,3653
    )

# Apply to all ECS log groups in production
for service in ['devlearner-api', 'devlearner-frontend', 'srs-processor']:
    set_log_retention(f'/ecs/{service}', retention_days=90)
examples.explanation: Custom metrics extend CloudWatch beyond AWS infrastructure metrics — record business KPIs (orders/minute, payment failure rate) in the same system as your infrastructure metrics. Composite Alarms solve alert fatigue: a single high CPU spike might not warrant a 3am page — but high CPU AND high error rate together do. EvaluationPeriods=2 with Period=300 means the alarm only fires after 10 continuous minutes above threshold, reducing false positives from brief spikes. CloudWatch Logs Insights lets you query log data with a SQL-like syntax — find slow endpoints, error patterns, or specific request IDs across millions of log lines in seconds. Log retention is not free — set it or you'll pay for logs from 2022 forever.
examples.pseudocode:
CloudWatch Alarm state machine:
  Metric: API 5xx errors
  Period: 300s, EvaluationPeriods: 2, Threshold: 1
  
  t=0s:  5xx = 0 (OK)
  t=300s: 5xx = 2% → period 1: BREACHING
  t=600s: 5xx = 1.5% → period 2: BREACHING
  → 2/2 periods breaching → state: OK → ALARM
  → AlarmAction: publish to SNS → PagerDuty pages on-call
  
  t=900s: 5xx = 0.1% → period: NOT BREACHING
  t=1200s: 5xx = 0.2% → period: NOT BREACHING
  → 2/2 periods OK → state: ALARM → OK
  → OKAction: publish to SNS → Slack notification
  
  TreatMissingData=notBreaching:
    If no data in period (Lambda not invoked) → treat as OK
    Prevents false alarms during quiet overnight periods
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner production monitoring: dashboard with 6 panels (ECS CPU/memory, ALB request count/5xx rate, RDS connections, SRS queue depth). Alarms: 5xx rate > 1% → SNS → Slack alert. ECS tasks < 2 (service degraded) → SNS → PagerDuty. Custom metric: SRS queue depth > 100 items/user (algorithm needs tuning). Logs Insights query: find all users who got 500 errors in the last hour for incident investigation.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  App[Spring Boot] -->|PutMetricData| CW[CloudWatch Custom Metric]
  ECS[ECS Tasks] -->|built-in| CWM[CloudWatch Metrics]
  ECS -->|awslogs driver| CWL[CloudWatch Logs]
  CWM --> Alarm[CloudWatch Alarm]
  CWL --> Insights[Logs Insights Query]
  Alarm -->|5xx > 1%| SNS[SNS Topic]
  SNS --> Slack[Slack Channel]
  SNS --> PD[PagerDuty]
  CWM --> Dashboard[CloudWatch Dashboard]
  Dashboard --> Screen[Ops Screen]
  CompAlarm[Composite Alarm: errors AND latency] --> SNS
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"description":"ECS task CPU spikes to 95% during traffic burst","state":"CloudWatch Metric: ECS/CPUUtilization = 95%"},{"step":2,"description":"Alarm: 1 period (5min) at 95% — not yet triggered (EvaluationPeriods=2)","state":"Alarm state: OK. Waiting for second consecutive period."},{"step":3,"description":"CPU stays high: second 5-minute period, CPU = 88%","state":"2/2 periods above 80% → Alarm state: OK → ALARM"},{"step":4,"description":"AlarmAction: SNS publish to pagerduty-alerts","state":"PagerDuty creates incident, pages on-call engineer"},{"step":5,"description":"Engineer investigates: runs Logs Insights query for slow requests","state":"Query finds: 15 requests with duration_ms > 5000ms, all to /api/search endpoint"},{"step":6,"description":"Traffic subsides, CPU drops to 20%","state":"2 consecutive periods below 80% → Alarm state: ALARM → OK"},{"step":7,"description":"OKAction: SNS to Slack","state":"Slack message: 'ECS CPU alarm resolved — CPU back to normal'"}]

problems.title: CloudFormation Change Set Analysis
problems.description: A CloudFormation change set for a production stack update shows: (1) Modify ECSService (no interruption). (2) Replace RDSInstance (requires replacement). (3) Modify SecurityGroup (no interruption). The team wants to apply this update. What should concern them, and what should they do before applying?
constraints: Replace = AWS deletes the old resource and creates a new one
RDS replacement: new instance has a new endpoint, no data migration built-in
CloudFormation does not automatically migrate data during replacement

examples.output: DANGER: RDS replacement deletes old instance (data lost unless backed up) and creates new with different endpoint. Action: 1. Take manual RDS snapshot. 2. Update app to handle new endpoint (change set will fail if endpoint is hardcoded). 3. Consider using RDS Blue/Green deployments instead of CloudFormation replacement.
examples.explanation: CloudFormation Replace is not a safe migration — for stateful resources like RDS, it means DELETE + CREATE, not in-place upgrade

hints: aws cloudformation describe-change-set shows which properties trigger replacement
RDS replacement triggers: changing DBInstanceClass, engine version (major), DBName, MasterUsername
Workaround: create new RDS instance manually, migrate data, update CloudFormation to import the new instance

problems.title: Set Up CloudWatch Alarm for ECS Service Health
problems.description: Create a CloudWatch alarm that fires when the number of running ECS tasks drops below 2 (service degraded). The alarm should send to an SNS topic. Configure it so that 1 evaluation period is sufficient (don't wait for 2 consecutive breaches) since task drop is always serious.

examples.input: ECS desired count=2, alert if running tasks < 2
examples.output: put_metric_alarm: MetricName=RunningTaskCount, Namespace=ECS/ContainerInsights, Threshold=2, ComparisonOperator=LessThanThreshold, EvaluationPeriods=1, Period=60
examples.explanation: Period=60 (1 minute) with EvaluationPeriods=1 means the alarm fires within 1 minute of a task drop — fast enough for a critical service

hints: ECS Container Insights must be enabled on the cluster to get RunningTaskCount metric
Dimensions: [{Name:ClusterName,Value:my-cluster},{Name:ServiceName,Value:devlearner-api-service}]
TreatMissingData=breaching: if no metric data, assume tasks are not running (conservative approach for critical alarms)

problems.title: Debug High CloudWatch Costs
problems.description: Your AWS bill shows $450/month for CloudWatch — much higher than expected. After investigation you find: 47 log groups with no retention policy, a Lambda function calling PutMetricData 10 times per invocation (invoked 1M times/month), and a detailed monitoring enabled on 200 EC2 instances. Identify the cost drivers and propose fixes.
constraints: Detailed EC2 monitoring: $2.10/instance/month vs $0 for basic

examples.input: 47 log groups, no retention. Lambda: 10 PutMetricData per invocation × 1M invocations. 200 EC2 detailed monitoring.
examples.output: Fix 1: Set 30-day retention on all log groups (delete old data: saves ~$100/mo if accumulating for years). Fix 2: Lambda: batch 10 metrics into 1 PutMetricData call (max 1000 data points per call) — same cost but more efficient. Fix 3: Disable detailed monitoring on non-critical EC2 instances: 200 × $2.10 = $420/mo → 20 critical × $2.10 = $42/mo, saves $378/mo.
examples.explanation: Detailed EC2 monitoring is the biggest win here: $420 → $42/mo. Log groups without retention are a slow leak — accumulate for years

hints: aws logs describe-log-groups | jq '.logGroups[] | select(.retentionInDays == null)' finds log groups without retention
PutMetricData: max 1000 MetricData items per call — batch to reduce API call frequency
aws ec2 describe-instances --filters 'Name=monitoring-state,Values=enabled' finds detailed monitoring instances
